draft.introduction: Eclogue 4, the “Messianic Eclogue,” prophesies the birth of a child who will inaugurate a new Golden Age. Christians from Constantine onward read it as a pagan prophecy of Christ, making Virgil a bridge between classical and Christian worlds.

translation.text: Now the last age of the Cumaean prophecy has come; the great order of the ages is born anew. Now the Virgin returns, the reign of Saturn returns; now a new generation descends from heaven on high. Only do you, pure Lucina, favor the child now being born, under whom the iron race shall first cease and a golden race spring up throughout the world.
translation.original_text: Ultima Cumaei venit iam carminis aetas; magnus ab integro saeclorum nascitur ordo. Iam redit et Virgo, redeunt Saturnia regna; iam nova progenies caelo demittitur alto.

translator_notes.note: The identity of the child has been debated since antiquity. Candidates include the expected offspring of Octavian or Pollio, or a purely symbolic figure. The Christian reading, championed by Constantine in his Oration to the Saints, transformed Virgil into a prophet despite himself.
